source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “You have conquered, O Gaul,” said Postumianus, “you have conquered, although certainly not me, who am, on the contrary, an upholder of Martin, and who have always known and believed all these things about that man; but you have conquered all the eremites and anchorites.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: For no one of them, like your friend, or rather our friend, Martin, ruled over deaths of all kinds.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And Sulpitius there justly compared him to the apostles and prophets, inasmuch as the power of his faith, and the works accomplished by his power, bear witness that he was, in all points, like them.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: But go on, I beg of you, although we can hear nothing more striking than we have heard—still, go on, O Gaul, to set forth what still remains of what you have to say concerning Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: For the mind is eager to know even the least and commonest of his doings, since there is no doubt that the least of his actions surpass the greatest deeds of others.”

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “I will do so,” replies the Gaul, “but I did not myself witness what I am about to relate, for it took place before I became an associate of Martin’s; still, the fact is well known, having been spread through the world by the accounts given by faithful brethren, who were present on the occasion.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Well, just about the time when he first became a bishop, a necessity arose for his visiting the imperial court.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Valentinian, the elder, then was at the head of affairs.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: When he came to know that Martin was asking for things which he did not incline to grant, he ordered him to be kept from entering the doors of the palace.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Besides his own unkind and haughty temper, his wife Arriana had urged him to this course, and had wholly alienated him from the holy man, so that he should not show him the regard which was due to him.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Martin, accordingly, when he had once and again endeavored to procure an interview with the haughty prince, had recourse to his well-known weapons—he clothes himself in sackcloth, scatters ashes upon his person, abstains from food and drink, and gives himself, night and day, to continuous prayer.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: On the seventh day, an angel appeared to him, and tells him to go with confidence to the palace, for that the royal doors, although closed against him, would open of their own accord, and that the haughty spirit of the emperor would be softened.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Martin, therefore, being encouraged by the address of the angel who thus appeared to him, and trusting to his assistance, went to the palace.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The doors stood open, and no one opposed his entrance; so that, going in, he came at last into the presence of the king, without any one seeking to hinder him.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The king, however, seeing him at a distance as he approached, and gnashing his teeth that he had been admitted, did not, by any means, condescend to rise up as Martin advanced, until fire covered the royal seat, and until the flames seized on a part of the royal person.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In this way the haughty monarch is driven from his throne, and, much against his will, rises up to receive Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He even gave many embraces to the man whom he had formerly determined to despise, and, coming to a better frame of mind, he confessed that he perceived the exercise of Divine power; without waiting even to listen to the requests of Martin, he granted all he desired before being asked.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Afterwards the king often invited the holy man both to conferences and entertainments; and, in the end, when he was about to depart, offered him many presents, which, however, the blessed man, jealously maintaining his own poverty, totally refused, as he did on all similar occasions.
